exercises.title: The Perspective-Taking Exercise
exercises.description: Choose someone you interacted with today (colleague, partner, stranger, service worker). Spend 3 minutes writing answers to these three questions from THEIR perspective.
hints: Don't judge their answers — this is about understanding, not evaluating
Consider their context: What pressures are they under? What's their background?
Be honest about what you don't know about their experience
questions: What might their day have been like before they interacted with me?
What emotions might they be carrying right now?
What do they need from this interaction that they might not say out loud?
exercises.solution: After 3 minutes of perspective-taking, you'll likely realize: (1) You know less about their experience than you assumed. (2) They might be dealing with pressures you can't see. (3) Your default assumption (usually self-focused) may be wrong. Practice this daily and your empathetic responses will become more natural.

exercises.title: The Empathy Response Rewrite
exercises.description: Rewrite these sympathy responses as empathetic responses. Focus on understanding, not fixing or minimizing.
hints: Replace 'I know how you feel' with curiosity
scenarios:
Friend: 'I failed my exam.'
Sympathetic response: 'At least you tried. You'll get it next time.'

Partner: 'I had a terrible day at work.'
Sympathetic response: 'Just quit that job. You deserve better.'

Colleague: 'I'm overwhelmed with this project.'
Sympathetic response: 'I know how you feel. Just make a to-do list.'

Family member: 'I'm so lonely since the move.'
Sympathetic response: 'Everything happens for a reason. You'll make friends soon.'
exercises.solution: Empathetic rewrites: (1) 'That's tough. What part was hardest?' (2) 'That sounds draining. What happened?' (3) 'I can see why — there's a lot on your plate. What's the heaviest piece?' (4) 'Moving is really hard. What do you miss most?' Notice: empathetic responses ask questions, acknowledge feelings, and don't rush to fix.

exercises.title: The 'What's That Like?' Challenge
exercises.description: For one week, use the phrase 'What's that like?' at least once per day in a conversation. Notice how it changes the depth of the conversation.
hints: Use it after someone shares an experience, opinion, or feeling
Follow up with genuine curiosity — not just polite interest
Notice how people respond when you ask this vs. when you give advice
exercises.solution: 'What's that like?' is the most empathetic question because it says: 'Your experience matters to me. I want to understand.' After a week, you'll notice: (1) People share more openly. (2) Conversations go deeper. (3) You learn things you never would have known. (4) Relationships strengthen. This single question can transform your communication.
